sections.title: 1. The 'Big Three' Questions
sections.description: The three simple questions used worldwide to measure financial literacy. They focus on:
list: Interest Rates: understanding how money grows in a bank account.
Inflation: knowing that as prices go up, your money buys less.
Risk: understanding that spreading money out is safer than one stock.

sections.title: 2. The Problem: Most People are Failing
sections.description: Financial knowledge is surprisingly low. In the U.S., only about 30% to 40% of adults can answer all three questions correctly.
list: Gender Gap: Women often score lower, partly due to lower confidence in their answers.
Age Gap: Knowledge peaks in the 50s; young people often have very little financial knowledge.
Inequality Gap: Minority backgrounds often have less access to info. For example, some studies show Black and Hispanic Americans scoring 10-15% lower on literacy tests due to systemic barriers.

sections.title: 3. Why This Matters to You
sections.description: Lack of understanding leads to expensive mistakes, such as:
list: Taking out student loans or credit cards you cannot afford.
Not saving enough for retirement.
Losing money on risky investments or 'get rich quick' schemes.

sections.title: 4. Simple Solutions: Schools and Jobs
sections.description: Money has become more complicated, so financial literacy should be treated like reading or writing.
list: Classes: High school personal finance classes lead to better financial decisions later in life.
Workplace Help: Employers are starting to offer wellness programs to reduce financial stress.

sections.title: 1. What is Career Readiness?
sections.description: Employability is about having the skills needed to get and keep a job. In the U.S., this is often called career readiness. It focuses on:
list: Communication: being able to express ideas clearly and listen effectively.
Critical Thinking: solving complex problems and making informed decisions.
Teamwork: collaborating well with diverse groups of people.
Responsibility: being reliable, ethical, and managing your tasks.
Technology: using the digital tools and systems needed for modern work.

sections.title: 2. The Shift: Schools & Jobs
sections.description: College is a major investment, and there is a growing expectation that schools must prove students are job-ready.
list: Rising Costs: Families want a clear return on investment (ROI) for their education.
Job Market Pressure: Employers often report that graduates aren't fully prepared for the workplace.
Changing Economy: The 'gig economy' and automation require more flexible, adaptable skills.

sections.title: 3. Why This Matters to You
sections.description: If students aren't career ready, they may face significant hurdles after graduation, including:
list: Difficulty finding or landing a first 'career-track' job.
Lacking the soft skills that managers value most for promotions.
Struggling to adapt as technology changes their specific industry over time.

sections.title: 4. The Challenges with Employability
sections.description: Focusing solely on 'employability' isn't always simple. There are systemic hurdles to consider:
list: Measurement: It is hard to prove someone has 'leadership' or 'ethics' through a standard test.
Equity Issues: Students from lower-income backgrounds often have less access to high-quality internships.
Outside Factors: Economic downturns can affect job outcomes regardless of how 'ready' a student is.

sections.title: 1. What is Media Literacy and Digital Citizenship?
sections.description: Understanding and using social media in a smart and responsible way focuses on:
list: Media Literacy: Understanding, analyzing, and creating media content.
Critical Thinking: Checking if information is true or biased.
Digital Citizenship: Using technology in a responsible and ethical way.
Online Behavior: Respecting others and staying safe online.

sections.title: 2. The Impact of Social Media
sections.description: Social media has fundamentally changed how we get and share information:
list: Fast Information: News and content spread across the globe instantly.
User Content: Anyone can create and share their own media.
Global Connection: Communicating worldwide is easier than ever.
Challenges: Navigating misinformation, privacy issues, and online harassment.

sections.title: 3. Why This Matters to You
sections.description: Without these skills, navigating the digital world can lead to significant struggles:
list: Believing false or misleading information (Fake News).
Sharing content without checking for accuracy.
Facing issues with personal privacy or online safety.
Engaging in negative or harmful online behavior.

sections.title: 4. The Challenges
sections.description: There are several critical problems to watch out for when using social media:
list: Misinformation: False information spreads faster than the truth.
Privacy Risks: Personal data can be exposed or misused.
Cyberbullying: Online harassment can deeply affect mental health.
Digital Divide: Not everyone has equal access to technology or the skills to use it.

footprintQuiz.q: Use the same password for multiple accounts?
Is your main social media set to Public?
Click links from unknown text messages?
Use public Wi-Fi for school logins?

finance_intro.title: Intro - What is Financial Literacy?
finance_intro.lecture: Financial literacy is the ability to understand how money works in the world. It involves three core concepts: Inflation (rising prices), Interest (the cost of borrowing or reward for saving), and Risk (the chance of loss). Mastering these helps you avoid debt and build wealth.
finance_intro.practice: Scenario: You find $100 in an old jacket and decide to hide it under your mattress for 10 years. What is the most likely outcome?

choices.text: The money will be worth more because it is safe.
choices.feedback: Actually, it will be worth less! While you still have $100, 'Inflation' means that $100 will buy fewer groceries in 10 years than it does today.

choices.text: The money will lose purchasing power over time.
choices.feedback: Correct! Because of inflation, the cost of goods rises, meaning 'stagnant' money loses its value.

choices.text: The money will stay exactly the same in value.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. The numerical value is $100, but its 'purchasing power' changes as prices rise.

finance_intro.quiz: Question: Which of the following best describes 'Inflation'?

quizChoices.text: The reward a bank gives you for saving money.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. That describes 'Interest'.

quizChoices.text: The general increase in prices and fall in the purchasing value of money.
quizChoices.feedback: Exactly! Inflation is why a candy bar costs more today than it did 20 years ago.

quizChoices.text: The chance that an investment will lose value.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. That is the definition of 'Risk'.

finance_using.title: Using Money
finance_using.lecture: Successful money management starts with a budget. A popular method is the 50/30/20 rule: 50% for Needs (Rent, Utilities), 30% for Wants (Entertainment, Hobbies), and 20% for Savings and Debt repayment.
finance_using.practice: Scenario: You have $1,000 for the month. Your rent is $500, groceries are $100, and you want a new $300 gaming monitor. According to the 50/30/20 rule, what should you do?

choices.text: Buy the monitor now since you have $400 left after rent.
choices.feedback: Careful! Spending $300 on a 'Want' leaves only $100 for your 20% savings goal ($200).

choices.text: Save $200 first, then see if you can afford the monitor.
choices.feedback: Correct! Following the 50/30/20 rule means prioritizing your savings before spending your full 'Wants' budget.

choices.text: Pay only half the rent so you can get the monitor and save.
choices.feedback: Definitely not! Rent is a 'Need' and failing to pay it can ruin your credit score.

finance_using.quiz: Question: In the 50/30/20 rule, what does the 20% represent?

quizChoices.text: Wants and Entertainment
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. Wants should take up roughly 30% of your budget.

quizChoices.text: Needs and Utilities
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. Needs represent the largest chunk at 50%.

quizChoices.text: Savings and Debt Repayment
quizChoices.feedback: Correct! Dedicating 20% to savings builds your financial safety net.

finance_decisions.title: Smart Decisions
finance_decisions.lecture: Smart financial decisions often involve understanding 'Opportunity Cost'—the value of what you give up when you choose one option over another. It also involves 'Compound Interest,' which is interest earned on interest.
finance_decisions.practice: Scenario: You have $50 a month you could spend on a premium coffee subscription or put into a Roth IRA (retirement account). If you start at age 18, why is the IRA considered the 'Smarter' decision?

choices.text: Because coffee is bad for your health.
choices.feedback: While health is important, the financial reason is 'Compound Interest.'

choices.text: Because of the power of Compound Interest over 40 years.
choices.feedback: Correct! By starting early, your interest earns its own interest, creating a snowball effect.

choices.text: Because the IRA gives you a higher immediate cash payout.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. An IRA is a long-term investment. The benefit is long-term growth, not immediate cash.

quizChoices.text: The monetary price tag on an item in a store.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. Opportunity cost is about choices, not just retail prices.

quizChoices.text: The value of the next best alternative you give up when making a choice.
quizChoices.feedback: Spot on! If you choose to study for an hour instead of working, your opportunity cost is the hourly wage you gave up.

quizChoices.text: A fee charged by banks for missing a payment.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. That is a late fee or penalty.

finance_earning.title: How to Make Money
finance_earning.lecture: Earning potential is tied to 'Human Capital'—your set of skills, education, and experience. Specialized skills that are in high demand but low supply typically command higher wages.
finance_earning.practice: Scenario: You are offered two summer jobs. Job A pays $20/hr to move boxes. Job B pays $15/hr but includes a certification course in Digital Marketing. Which choice increases your long-term 'Human Capital'?

choices.text: Job A, because $20 is more than $15.
choices.feedback: Short-term, yes. But Job B offers a certification that could lead to a much higher-paying career later.

choices.text: Job B, because the certification adds a specialized skill.
choices.feedback: Correct! Investing in your skills is the most reliable way to increase your lifetime earning potential.

choices.text: Neither, you should wait for a job that pays $25/hr.
choices.feedback: Waiting without building skills doesn't increase your value to employers.

finance_earning.quiz: Question: Which of the following is an example of 'Human Capital'?

quizChoices.text: The cash you have stored in your wallet.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. That is financial capital.

quizChoices.text: A bilingual employee's ability to speak both English and Spanish.
quizChoices.feedback: Exactly! Skills, education, and languages are prime examples of human capital.

quizChoices.text: The computers and machinery owned by a company.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. Those are physical assets or physical capital.

career_readiness.title: Career Readiness & NACE Competencies
career_readiness.lecture: Career readiness is the foundation for a successful transition into the workplace. Employers look for core competencies like Critical Thinking, Communication, Teamwork, and Professionalism.
career_readiness.practice: Scenario: Your boss asks you to solve a problem with a difficult customer, but doesn't give you exact step-by-step instructions. How do you apply 'Critical Thinking'?

choices.text: Wait for the boss to tell you exactly what to do.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Employers value initiative.

choices.text: Tell the customer you can't help them.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. This is unprofessional and fails to resolve the issue.

choices.text: Analyze the issue, brainstorm solutions, and present the best one to your boss.
choices.feedback: Correct! Critical thinking involves analyzing a situation independently and formulating a logical solution.

career_readiness.quiz: Question: Which of the following is considered a core career readiness competency?

quizChoices.text: Having the most expensive professional wardrobe.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. Professionalism is about behavior and reliability, not just expensive clothes.

quizChoices.text: Effective Communication.
quizChoices.feedback: Yes! The ability to clearly articulate thoughts in written and oral forms is highly sought after by employers.

quizChoices.text: Never asking questions on the job.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. Asking clarifying questions is a sign of engagement and good communication.

budgeting_basics.title: The Art of Budgeting
budgeting_basics.lecture: A budget is a plan that allocates your future income toward expenses, savings, and debt repayment. To work, financial goals must be 'SMART': specific, realistic, and have a clear timeframe.
budgeting_basics.practice: Scenario: You want to save $600 for a summer trip in 6 months. You earn $300 a month from a part-time job. What is the best way to budget for this?

choices.text: Hope you have $600 left over by the time summer starts.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. This isn't a plan; 'hope' is not a budget.

choices.text: Set aside $100 every month specifically for the trip.
choices.feedback: Correct! This is a specific, realistic goal with a clear timeframe.

choices.text: Spend your whole check and put the trip on a credit card later.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. This creates debt rather than using a budget to reach a goal.

budgeting_basics.quiz: Question: What is a personal budget primarily designed to do?

quizChoices.text: To stop you from ever spending money on fun things.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. A budget allows for fun by planning for it!

quizChoices.text: To show you how much money your neighbors are making.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. A budget is personal and focused on your own income.

quizChoices.text: To allocate your income toward expenses and savings based on a plan.
quizChoices.feedback: Correct! It tells your money where to go.

banking_services.title: Banking & Hidden Fees
banking_services.lecture: Banks offer many services, but they also charge fees. Small, frequent costs like ATM fees can drain your savings over time. Wise banking means choosing products with the lowest costs and highest benefits.
banking_services.practice: Scenario: You use an out-of-network ATM twice a week, and each time it costs you $1. At the end of the year, you've spent over $100. If you had invested that instead, it could have grown significantly. What should you do?

choices.text: Keep using the ATM because $1 doesn't seem like a lot.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Small amounts add up to large sums over time.

choices.text: Find an ATM within your bank's network to avoid the fee.
choices.feedback: Correct! Avoiding small fees is a simple way to protect your wealth.

choices.text: Stop using banks entirely and keep cash in a box.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Banks offer security and interest; you just have to use them wisely.

banking_services.quiz: Question: If you save $2 a week by avoiding fees and invest it at 5% interest, what is the likely outcome after five years?

quizChoices.text: You will have exactly $10 in total.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. You'd have much more than that.

quizChoices.text: The money would grow to over $500.
quizChoices.feedback: Correct! Compound interest and consistency turn small savings into large amounts.

quizChoices.text: You would lose money because of inflation.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. While inflation exists, the investment growth outweighs the small fee cost.

credit_reputation.title: Credit: Your Financial Reputation
credit_reputation.lecture: Credit is your ability to borrow money with the promise to pay it back. Using it wisely is critical to building a solid credit history. A good history makes it easier to rent apartments, buy cars, or get low-interest loans.
credit_reputation.practice: Scenario: You borrow money for a new laptop and miss two payments because you forgot. How does this affect your future?

choices.text: It doesn't matter as long as you pay it eventually.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Late payments are recorded in your credit history and hurt your reputation.

choices.text: It makes future loans more expensive or harder to get.
choices.feedback: Correct! Lenders see missed payments as a sign of high risk.

choices.text: The bank will just give you more money to help you out.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Banks are less likely to lend to those with poor payment histories.

credit_reputation.quiz: Question: What is the most critical factor in maintaining 'fiscal fitness' regarding credit?

quizChoices.text: Having as many credit cards as possible.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. More cards often lead to more debt.

quizChoices.text: Using credit wisely and paying it back on time.
quizChoices.feedback: Correct! A solid history is built on reliability.

quizChoices.text: Never using credit for anything at all.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. You need to use credit responsibly to build a history.

car_ownership.title: Cars and Loans
car_ownership.lecture: The cost of a car is more than just the monthly loan payment. You must also budget for 'operating costs,' including gas, insurance, and maintenance, which are often underestimated.
car_ownership.practice: Scenario: You can afford a $250 monthly car payment. You find a car that fits that budget perfectly. However, you haven't checked the insurance cost yet. What is the risk?

choices.text: There is no risk; the car payment is all that matters.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Insurance and gas could double your monthly cost.

choices.text: The total cost of ownership might exceed your monthly budget.
choices.feedback: Correct! You must calculate the 'real' cost, not just the loan.

choices.text: You should buy the car and skip the insurance to save money.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Insurance is legally required and financially necessary.

car_ownership.quiz: Question: What are typical 'operating costs' associated with a vehicle?

quizChoices.text: Only the price of the steering wheel.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. That's part of the car's initial price.

quizChoices.text: Gas, insurance, and regular maintenance.
quizChoices.feedback: Correct! These are ongoing costs of keeping a car on the road.

quizChoices.text: The cost of the driver's license only.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. A license is a separate personal cost.

saving_investing.title: Saving and Investing
saving_investing.lecture: Saving is setting money aside for the future; investing is putting that money to work to earn a return. Small amounts saved regularly can grow into large sums thanks to time and interest.
saving_investing.practice: Scenario: You start saving just 35 cents a day in a jar. After one year, you have over $125. If you put that into a savings account that earns interest, what happens next?

choices.text: The money will stay at $125 forever.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Interest helps the money grow over time.

choices.text: The money will grow even faster because of interest on your savings.
choices.feedback: Correct! Saving is the first step; interest is the second.

choices.text: You will lose the money because the bank will spend it.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Your savings are protected and earn you money.

saving_investing.quiz: Question: What is the first step in the saving and investing process?

quizChoices.text: Waiting until you are rich to start.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. Starting early with small amounts is better.

quizChoices.text: Setting clear financial goals.
quizChoices.feedback: Correct! You need to know what you are saving for to stay committed.

quizChoices.text: Borrowing money to invest in the stock market.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. You should save your own money first.

bonds_basics.title: Understanding Bonds
bonds_basics.lecture: A bond is essentially a loan you give to a government or a company. In exchange, they promise to pay you back the full amount on a specific 'maturity date' and give you regular interest payments along the way. It is generally seen as a way to raise funds for big projects.
bonds_basics.practice: Scenario: A city wants to build a new high school and 'sells bonds' to the public. You buy a $1,000 bond that pays 5% interest yearly for 10 years. What happens after 10 years?

choices.text: The city keeps your $1,000 but keeps paying you interest forever.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Bonds have a 'maturity date' where the original loan is repaid and interest stops.

choices.text: The city pays you back your $1,000 principal in full.
choices.feedback: Correct! At the end of the term, you get your initial investment back plus all the interest you earned over the years.

choices.text: You now own a small piece of the high school building.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. That sounds like a stock. Bonds are loans, not ownership.

bonds_basics.quiz: Question: What is the 'principal' of a bond?

quizChoices.text: The person in charge of the company or government.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. In finance, principal refers to the money amount.

quizChoices.text: The original loan amount that is repaid at the end of the term.
quizChoices.feedback: Correct! The principal is the base amount you lent them.

quizChoices.text: The interest rate the bond pays every year.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. That is the 'coupon rate' or interest rate.

diversification_index.title: Diversification & Index Funds
diversification_index.lecture: Diversification is the strategy of spreading your money across many different investments to reduce risk—don't put all your eggs in one basket! An Index Fund makes this easy by automatically buying a tiny piece of every company in a market index (like the S&P 500) for a very low fee.
diversification_index.practice: Scenario: You have $1,000. You put it all into one brand-new tech company. Two weeks later, that company goes bankrupt. What is the better way to have invested that money?

choices.text: Buy an Index Fund that spreads the $1,000 across 500 different companies.
choices.feedback: Correct! If one company in an index fund fails, the other 499 still protect your investment. That is diversification.

choices.text: Keep the money in cash under your bed to avoid all risk.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. While safe from bankruptcy, you lose value to inflation and miss out on growth.

choices.text: Wait for a different tech company to look 'lucky' and try again.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Relying on luck in a single stock is high-risk gambling, not diversifying.

diversification_index.quiz: Question: Why do Index Funds typically have lower fees than 'Hedge Funds'?

quizChoices.text: Because they are managed by the government.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. They are private investments.

quizChoices.text: Because they aren't 'actively managed'; they just track a specific list of companies.
quizChoices.feedback: Exactly! Since there isn't a team picking individual stocks all day, the costs stay low.

quizChoices.text: Because they only invest in small, cheap companies.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. Index funds can track the biggest companies in the world.

stocks_portfolios.title: Stocks & Investment Portfolios
stocks_portfolios.lecture: A stock represents partial ownership in a corporation. If the company profits, you own a slice of that success. An 'Investment Portfolio' is simply the total collection of all your assets—stocks, bonds, cash, and even things like real estate or artwork.
stocks_portfolios.practice: Scenario: You own 10 shares of a shoe company. The company releases a huge hit sneaker and their profits double. How does this affect your 'portfolio'?

choices.text: It doesn't affect you because you only own a small part.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. As a partial owner (shareholder), you benefit when the company's value rises.

choices.text: The value of your stocks in that company will likely increase, raising your portfolio's total value.
choices.feedback: Correct! Your portfolio grows because your ownership in that profitable company is now worth more.

choices.text: The company will send you free shoes.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. While nice, stocks grant you a portion of assets and profits, not necessarily free products!

stocks_portfolios.quiz: Question: What is the difference between a 'Stock' and a 'Portfolio'?

quizChoices.text: A stock is an ownership slice; a portfolio is your entire collection of investments.
quizChoices.feedback: Perfect! Think of a stock as an ingredient and the portfolio as the whole meal.

quizChoices.text: A stock is for rich people; a portfolio is for students.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. Anyone can start a portfolio with even one stock.

quizChoices.text: There is no difference; the terms mean the same thing.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. One is a single asset; the other is the total collection.

career_soft_skills.title: Soft Skills & Professionalism
career_soft_skills.lecture: Hard skills are specific technical abilities (like coding or cooking), but 'Soft Skills' are interpersonal attributes (like time management, adaptability, and emotional intelligence). Professionalism combines these skills with reliability.
career_soft_skills.practice: Scenario: You make a mistake on a group project that costs your team points. What is the most professional way to handle it?

choices.text: Blame the instructions for being confusing.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Deflecting blame shows a lack of accountability.

choices.text: Own up to the mistake, apologize, and offer a plan to fix it.
choices.feedback: Correct! Taking responsibility demonstrates high emotional intelligence and professionalism.

choices.text: Ignore it and hope no one notices.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Ignoring mistakes breaks trust within a team.

career_soft_skills.quiz: Question: Which of the following is an example of a 'Soft Skill'?

quizChoices.text: Writing code in HTML and CSS.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. Coding is a technical 'Hard Skill'.

quizChoices.text: Changing the oil in a 2018 vehicle.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. Mechanical knowledge is a 'Hard Skill'.

quizChoices.text: Adaptability in a fast-changing work environment.
quizChoices.feedback: Perfect! Adaptability, communication, and leadership are all crucial soft skills.

career_pathways.title: Discovering Your Career Path
career_pathways.lecture: Choosing a career path involves aligning your interests, strengths, and values with market demand. It is completely normal to change careers multiple times. Networking, internships, and informational interviews are great ways to explore.
career_pathways.practice: Scenario: You are interested in graphic design but aren't sure if you want to do it for a living. What is the best next step?

choices.text: Give up because you don't have professional experience yet.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Everyone starts with zero experience!

choices.text: Take out a large loan for an art degree immediately.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. It is risky to make a massive financial commitment before you even know if you enjoy the work.

choices.text: Ask a professional designer for a 15-minute informational interview.
choices.feedback: Correct! Informational interviews allow you to ask questions about the reality of a job before committing to it.

career_pathways.quiz: Question: What is the primary purpose of an informational interview?

quizChoices.text: To demand that a company hire you immediately.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. That approach is too aggressive and misses the point of learning.

quizChoices.text: To learn about a specific career field from someone currently working in it.
quizChoices.feedback: Exactly! It's a low-pressure way to gather real-world insights and expand your network.

quizChoices.text: To practice negotiating a salary.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. You are not interviewing for an open position during an informational interview.

apprenticeships.title: Learning a Trade
apprenticeships.lecture: An apprenticeship is like a 'job with a teacher.' Instead of paying a college to sit in a desk, a company pays YOU to learn a skill like plumbing, electrical work, or car repair. You spend some time in a classroom and most of your time doing the actual work for a paycheck.
apprenticeships.practice: Scenario: You want to be a master electrician. You join a program where you work 4 days a week with a pro and go to class 1 day a week. You are getting paid while you learn. What is this?

choices.text: A standard college degree.
choices.feedback: Nope! In college, you usually pay them. In an apprenticeship, they pay you.

choices.text: An apprenticeship.
choices.feedback: Exactly! It’s a paid way to become an expert in a 'skilled trade'.

choices.text: A volunteer hobby.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. This is a professional career path where you earn real money.

apprenticeships.quiz: Question: Which of these is a 'skilled trade' that uses an apprenticeship?

quizChoices.text: Construction Electrician
quizChoices.feedback: Correct! This is a job that requires hands-on training and expert knowledge.

quizChoices.text: Working at a fast-food counter
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. While it's a job, it doesn't usually require a years-long apprenticeship.

quizChoices.text: Playing video games
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. That's a hobby, not a vocational trade.

career_assessments.title: Finding Your 'Vibe'
career_assessments.lecture: A career assessment is a personality test for your future. It asks questions about what you like (talking to people vs. working alone) and what you value (money vs. helping others). The goal is to give you a list of jobs that actually fit your personality.
career_assessments.practice: Scenario: You take a test that says you hate being stuck inside and you love solving puzzles. It suggests you'd be a great 'Game Warden' or 'Engineer.' Why did you take this test?

choices.text: To get a grade for my report card.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. This test isn't for a grade; it's to help you find a career path.

choices.text: To find jobs that match my personality and interests.
choices.feedback: Spot on! It helps narrow down your choices so you don't pick a job you'll hate.

choices.text: Because the computer wanted to guess my age.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. It's looking for your interests, not your age.

career_assessments.quiz: Question: Who can help you understand what your test results mean?

quizChoices.text: A school counselor or career coach.
quizChoices.feedback: Yes! They can help you turn those results into a real-world plan.

quizChoices.text: A random person on the bus.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. You want a professional who knows how these tests work.

quizChoices.text: No one; the test is just for fun.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. These are tools to help you make big life decisions!

learning_plans.title: Picking Your Own Classes
learning_plans.lecture: An Individualized Learning Plan (ILP) is a map for high school. If you know you want to be a nurse, you don't want to take four years of choir—you want extra science and health classes. An ILP helps you pick the right classes now to reach your goal later.
learning_plans.practice: Scenario: You want to be a YouTuber or a Filmmaker. You talk to your teacher and swap your 'Home Ec' class for 'Digital Media and Video Editing.' What are you doing?

choices.text: Trying to get an easy 'A'.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. You are picking a class that actually helps your future career.

choices.text: Creating a Learning Plan (ILP).
choices.feedback: Correct! You are matching your schoolwork to your future dreams.

choices.text: Avoiding graduation.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. This actually helps you graduate with useful skills!

learning_plans.quiz: Question: What is the main point of an ILP?

quizChoices.text: To make sure you take classes that help you with your future job.
quizChoices.feedback: Correct! It keeps you on track for college or your career.

quizChoices.text: To get out of doing math.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. You still have to do the basics, but you add classes you care about.

quizChoices.text: To decide what you're eating for lunch.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. It’s an academic plan, not a menu.

internships.title: Testing a Career
internships.lecture: An internship is like a 'trial run' for a job. You work at a company for a few weeks or months to see if you actually like it. It’s the best way to see what a job is *really* like before you spend years studying for it.
internships.practice: Scenario: You think you want to be a lawyer, so you intern at a law firm. You realize you hate reading long papers and being in an office all day. Is this internship a failure?

choices.text: Yes, because you wasted your time.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. You learned something very important!

choices.text: No, because you found out that career isn't for you before you spent money on law school.
choices.feedback: Exactly! Knowing what you DON'T want to do is just as important as knowing what you do.

choices.text: Yes, because you didn't get a paycheck.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Even if it's unpaid, the knowledge you gained is worth a lot of money.

internships.quiz: Question: What is one of the biggest benefits of an internship?

quizChoices.text: Getting a fancy job title to show your friends.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. The real value is the experience and connections.

quizChoices.text: Building a 'network' (meeting people who can help you get a job later).
quizChoices.feedback: Correct! Meeting professionals is a huge help for your future.

quizChoices.text: Learning how to use the company vending machine.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. That's a perk, but not a career skill!

soft_skills.title: Being a Good Teammate
soft_skills.lecture: Soft skills are your 'people skills.' They aren't about how well you use a computer; they are about how well you talk to others, lead a team, and handle stress. Every single job in the world requires these.
soft_skills.practice: Scenario: A customer is angry because their food is cold. You listen to them, apologize, and stay calm while you fix the problem. What skill are you using?

choices.text: A technical skill like cooking.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Cooking the food is technical; handling the person is a 'soft skill'.

choices.text: A soft skill like communication or problem-solving.
choices.feedback: Correct! These skills help you work well with customers and coworkers.

choices.text: A math skill.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. This is about people, not numbers.

soft_skills.quiz: Question: Why are soft skills important for your future?

quizChoices.text: Because they make you better at any job you ever have.
quizChoices.feedback: Right! Whether you are a doctor or a plumber, you need to work well with others.

quizChoices.text: Because they are the only thing you need to get hired.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. You usually need 'hard skills' too, but soft skills keep the job.

quizChoices.text: They aren't important; only your grades matter.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. Employers often care more about your attitude and teamwork than your GPA.

digital_media_literacy.title: Media Literacy & Fact Checking
digital_media_literacy.lecture: Media literacy is the ability to critically evaluate the information you consume online. It involves checking sources, recognizing clickbait, and cross-referencing facts with reliable organizations before sharing.
digital_media_literacy.practice: Scenario: You see a viral social media post claiming that a new law will ban all video games next month. What should you do before sharing it?

choices.text: Share it immediately so your friends are warned.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Sharing unverified information contributes to the spread of misinformation.

choices.text: Search for the claim on reputable news sites or fact-checking websites.
choices.feedback: Correct! Always verify sensational claims through trusted sources before sharing.

choices.text: Comment angrily on the post.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Engaging with the post pushes the content to more people.

digital_media_literacy.quiz: Question: What does it mean to cross-reference a fact?

quizChoices.text: Only reading the headline before sharing an article.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. Headlines are often misleading clickbait.

quizChoices.text: Checking multiple, independent sources to verify if a claim is actually true.
quizChoices.feedback: Correct! If three different reliable sources report the same facts, it is much more likely to be accurate.

quizChoices.text: Deleting an article if you disagree with it.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. That is censorship, not fact-checking.

digital_privacy.title: Protecting Privacy & Info
digital_privacy.lecture: Your 'digital footprint' is the trail of data you leave behind online. Protecting your privacy involves using strong, unique passwords, enabling Two-Factor Authentication (2FA), and being cautious about what personal details you share publicly.
digital_privacy.practice: Scenario: You are creating an account on a new social media app. It asks for your full name, phone number, home address, and school. How should you proceed?

choices.text: Fill out everything so people can find you easily.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Providing excessive personal information makes you vulnerable to identity theft.

choices.text: Use your bank password so you don't forget it.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Never reuse passwords! If the app gets hacked, your bank is at risk.

choices.text: Provide only the required fields and keep your profile set to private.
choices.feedback: Correct! Minimizing the data you share limits your digital footprint.

digital_privacy.quiz: Question: What is the primary benefit of Two-Factor Authentication (2FA)?

quizChoices.text: It requires a second form of verification (like a text code) making it much harder for hackers to access your account.
quizChoices.feedback: Yes! Even if a hacker guesses your password, they still can't get in without your physical phone.

quizChoices.text: It makes your internet connection run twice as fast.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. 2FA is for security, not speed.

quizChoices.text: It stops all spam emails from reaching your inbox.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. Spam filters handle that, not 2FA.

digital_bias.title: Identifying Media Bias
digital_bias.lecture: Bias is a disproportionate weight in favor of or against an idea. Algorithms on social media create 'filter bubbles' by showing you content that confirms your existing beliefs. Recognizing this helps you seek out diverse perspectives.
digital_bias.practice: Scenario: You notice your social media feed only shows news articles that support your favorite sports team and mocks the rival team. What is happening?

choices.text: The algorithm has created a filter bubble based on your past clicks and likes.
choices.feedback: Correct! Social media platforms are designed to keep you engaged by feeding you information you already agree with.

choices.text: Everyone in the world suddenly agrees with you.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Your feed is just curating a very specific slice of the internet.

choices.text: The rival team has stopped generating any news.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. The news exists, but the algorithm is choosing not to show to you.

digital_bias.quiz: Question: What is a 'filter bubble' in the context of social media?

quizChoices.text: A physical screen protector that prevents glare on your phone.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. That is a hardware accessory.

quizChoices.text: A feature that automatically deletes offensive comments.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. That is a moderation tool.

quizChoices.text: A state of intellectual isolation resulting from algorithms serving you only information you already agree with.
quizChoices.feedback: Exactly! Escaping the bubble requires actively searching for different viewpoints.

info_literacy.title: Spotting Fake News
info_literacy.lecture: Not everything you read online is true. Information Literacy is the skill of checking if a website is credible (trustworthy) or just making things up. Before you believe a post or use it for a school project, you should check who wrote it and where they got their facts.
info_literacy.practice: Scenario: You find an article claiming that drinking pickle juice gives you superpowers. The website has tons of pop-up ads and was written by 'Anonymous.' Should you use this for your science report?

choices.text: Yes, because it’s on the Internet so it must be true.
choices.feedback: Incorrect! Anyone can make a website. You need to look for high-quality, credible sources.

choices.text: No, I should check other trusted websites to see if they say the same thing.
choices.feedback: Correct! This is called 'cross-checking,' and it's a great way to see if information is accurate.

choices.text: Yes, but only if the website looks fancy.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Even a pretty website can have fake information. Always check the author and the facts.

quizChoices.text: The ability to read really fast.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. Speed doesn't matter if the info is wrong!

quizChoices.text: The skill of evaluating if information is accurate and trustworthy.
quizChoices.feedback: Exactly! It's about being a smart detective when you're online.

quizChoices.text: Knowing how to turn on a computer.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. That's a basic tech skill, not information literacy.

digital_ethics.title: Giving Credit Online
digital_ethics.lecture: Copying text from a website without saying where you got it is called plagiarism—it's basically stealing. Just like with books, you must cite (give credit to) the person who created the images or words you use online.
digital_ethics.practice: Scenario: You are writing an essay and find a perfect paragraph on a blog. You copy and paste it into your paper but don't mention the blog. What is this called?

choices.text: Smart researching.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. If you don't give credit, it's considered academic dishonesty.

choices.text: Plagiarism.
choices.feedback: Correct! Copying from a website is the same as stealing from a book. Always cite your sources!

choices.text: Creative sharing.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. You can share, but you still have to say who the original owner is.

digital_ethics.quiz: Question: What should you do if you use a photo or quote from a website for a project?

quizChoices.text: Nothing, if it's on Google it's free for everyone.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. Most things online are 'intellectual property' and belong to someone else.

quizChoices.text: Properly reference or cite where the information came from.
quizChoices.feedback: Correct! This shows respect for the creator and avoids plagiarism.

quizChoices.text: Change one or two words so it's 'yours'.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. That is still considered plagiarism.

digital_footprint.title: Your Digital Footprint
digital_footprint.lecture: Every time you post a photo, write a tweet, or even search for something, you leave a 'digital footprint.' This is a permanent trail of data that stays online forever. Even if you 'delete' a post, someone might have screenshotted it.
digital_footprint.practice: Scenario: You are frustrated with a teacher and post an angry comment on social media. You delete it 10 minutes later. Is it gone forever?

choices.text: Yes, once it’s deleted, it’s completely erased from the internet.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Data can be saved by others or stored on servers long after you hit delete.

choices.text: No, it’s likely part of your digital footprint and could be found later.
choices.feedback: Correct! You should never assume anything is private once you post it online.

choices.text: Yes, as long as your account is set to private.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Privacy settings help, but they aren't 100% perfect.

digital_footprint.quiz: Question: What makes up your 'Digital Footprint'?

quizChoices.text: Only the things you intentionally post on Instagram or TikTok.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. It also includes passive things like your browsing history and IP address.

quizChoices.text: Everything you do online, including posts, searches, and cookies.
quizChoices.feedback: Exactly! It’s the total record of your online activity.

quizChoices.text: The physical size of your phone.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. That has nothing to do with your digital data trail.

internet_safety.title: Protecting Yourself Online
internet_safety.lecture: Internet safety is about keeping your private info private. This means using strong passwords (not 'password123'), turning on privacy settings, and being careful about sharing things like your location or phone number.
internet_safety.practice: Scenario: A new 'friend' you met in a gaming lobby asks for your phone number and the name of your school so they can 'hang out.' What should you do?

choices.text: Give it to them so you can make a new friend.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. You should never give personal info to strangers online.

choices.text: Tell them you don't share personal info and keep your settings private.
choices.feedback: Correct! Protecting your identity is the first rule of internet safety.

choices.text: Give them a fake number just to be nice.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. It's better to just not engage and keep your info secure.

internet_safety.quiz: Question: Which of these is a 'strong' password habit?

quizChoices.text: Using your birthday so you don't forget it.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. Birthdays are easy for hackers to guess.

quizChoices.text: Using a mix of letters, numbers, and symbols that aren't easy to guess.
quizChoices.feedback: Correct! Complex passwords are much harder to hack.

quizChoices.text: Using the same password for every single account.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. If one account gets hacked, they all get hacked!

cyberbullying_prevention.title: Stopping Cyberbullying
cyberbullying_prevention.lecture: Cyberbullying is using technology to harass or mean to others. About 34% of students have experienced it. It’s important to speak up, report harassment, and never join in on the bullying.
cyberbullying_prevention.practice: Scenario: You see a group chat where everyone is making fun of a classmate and sharing embarrassing photos of them. What is the best thing to do?

choices.text: Stay quiet so you don't become the next target.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Staying quiet lets the bullying continue. Being an 'upstander' is better.

choices.text: Report the behavior to a trusted adult and don't participate.
choices.feedback: Correct! Addressing it can stop the current bully and prevent future harm.

choices.text: Join in so the group thinks you're cool.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. This makes you a cyberbully too and has serious consequences.

quizChoices.text: When your computer gets a virus.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. That's a technical issue, not a social one.

quizChoices.text: Using technology to harass, threaten, or embarrass someone else.
quizChoices.feedback: Correct! It is a serious issue that affects many students today.

quizChoices.text: Being better at a video game than someone else.
quizChoices.feedback: Incorrect. Competition is fine; harassment is not.
